---
title: "Confidence intervals"
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    theme: "default"
    colortheme: "default"
    fonttheme: "default"
  ioslides_presentation: default
  slidy_presentation: default
header-includes:
- \usepackage{enumerate}
- \usepackage{hyperref}
- \usepackage{caption}
- \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
- \definecolor{links}{HTML}{2A1B81}
- \hypersetup{colorlinks,linkcolor=,urlcolor=links}
colorlinks: true
linkcolor: blue
urlcolor: blue
---

```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE);
set.seed(20260226);
```



## Confidence in a sampled mean value

\Huge 

- How certain are we that our sample mean ($\bar{x}$) is close to the population mean ($\mu_{x}$)?
- Should express our uncertainty clearly
- **Confidence intervals** reflect uncertainty of $\mu_{x}$


## Confidence in a sampled mean value

\Huge 

**Confidence in $\mathbf{\mu_{x}}$:**

- **High**: Mean wing length was $\bar{x} = 7.2\:cm\:\pm0.1$
- **Low**: Mean wing length was $\bar{x} = 7.2\:cm\:\pm2.0$

Larger $\pm$ interval means *less* confidence in the true mean



## Confidence in a sampled mean value

\Huge 

**Confidence in $\mathbf{\mu_{x}}$:**

- **High**: Mean wing length was $\bar{x} = 7.2\:cm\:(7.1, 7.3)$
- **Low**: Mean wing length was $\bar{x} = 7.2\:cm\:(5.2, 9.2)$


Show the lower (7.1) and upper (7.3) confidence interval (CI)


## Confidence in a sampled mean value

\Huge 

**The goal of CIs is to contain uncertainty:**

- How frequently will the CIs that we draw *contain* the population mean ($\mu_{x}$)?
- Logic is again based on repeated resampling



## Sampling from a population (confidence intervals)

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "30+ bird silhouettes in a dotted box called 'population'. Within this, four birds are in a grey box called 'sample'.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/population_sample_1.png")
```



## Consider the distribution of a sample of N = 40


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "40 bird silhouettes in a grey box called 'sample'.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/just_sample.png")
```


## Sample means given true mean of 7.2 when N = 40

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Grey boxes each with 4 birds representing samples with different average heights.", fig.cap = "", out.width="95%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/samples_wing_40.png")
```


## Confidence in a sampled mean value (80%)

```{r, echo = FALSE}
samps_mn <- rep(x = NA, times = 20);
samp_mn  <- rep(x = NA, times = 20);
samp_se  <- rep(x = NA, times = 20);
for(i in 1:20){
  the_samp    <- rnorm(n = 40, mean = 1, sd = 1);
  std_err     <- sd(the_samp) / sqrt(40);
  samp_mn[i]  <- mean(the_samp);
  samp_se[i]  <- std_err;
}
samp_mn <- samp_mn + 6.2
offset  <- c(rep(x = 0, times = 4), rep(x = -1000, times = 16))
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
plot(x = 1:20, y = samp_mn + offset, ylim = c(6.3, 7.9), pch = 20, cex = 3, 
     xaxt = "n",
     xlab = "Sample from the population (N = 40)", ylab = "Mean of the sample",
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2);
axis(side = 1, at = 1:20, labels = 1:20, cex.lab = 1.75);
abline(h = 7.2, lwd = 3, lty = "dashed", col = "blue");
for(i in 1:4){
  arrows(x0 = i, x1 = i, y0 = samp_mn[i], y1 = samp_mn[i] - (1.28 * samp_se[i]), 
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.1);
  arrows(x0 = i, x1 = i, y0 = samp_mn[i], y1 = samp_mn[i] + (1.28 * samp_se[i]),
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.1);
}
text(x = 4, y = 7.88, labels = "Population mean = 7.2", cex = 1.75, 
     col = "blue");
```



## Confidence in a sampled mean value (80%)

```{r, echo = FALSE}
samps_mn <- rep(x = NA, times = 20);
samp_mn  <- rep(x = NA, times = 20);
samp_se  <- rep(x = NA, times = 20);
for(i in 1:20){
  the_samp    <- rnorm(n = 40, mean = 1, sd = 1);
  std_err     <- sd(the_samp) / sqrt(40);
  samp_mn[i]  <- mean(the_samp);
  samp_se[i]  <- std_err;
}
samp_mn <- samp_mn + 6.2
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
plot(x = 1:20, y = samp_mn, ylim = c(6.3, 7.9), pch = 20, cex = 3, xaxt = "n",
     xlab = "Sample from the population (N = 40)", ylab = "Mean of the sample",
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2);
axis(side = 1, at = 1:20, labels = 1:20, cex.lab = 1.75);
abline(h = 7.2, lwd = 3, lty = "dashed", col = "blue");
for(i in 1:20){
  arrows(x0 = i, x1 = i, y0 = samp_mn[i], y1 = samp_mn[i] - (1.28 * samp_se[i]), 
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.1);
  arrows(x0 = i, x1 = i, y0 = samp_mn[i], y1 = samp_mn[i] + (1.28 * samp_se[i]),
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.1);
}
text(x = 4, y = 7.88, labels = "Population mean = 7.2", cex = 1.75, 
     col = "blue");
```


## Confidence in a sampled mean value

\Huge 

**The goal of CIs is to contain uncertainty:**

- How frequently will the CIs that we draw *contain* the population mean ($\mu_{x}$)?
- **Having higher confidence requires wider intervals**



## Confidence in a sampled mean value (95%)

```{r, echo = FALSE}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
plot(x = 1:20, y = samp_mn, ylim = c(6.3, 7.9), pch = 20, cex = 3, xaxt = "n",
     xlab = "Sample from the population (N = 40)", ylab = "Mean of the sample",
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2);
axis(side = 1, at = 1:20, labels = 1:20, cex.lab = 1.75);
abline(h = 7.2, lwd = 3, lty = "dashed", col = "blue");
for(i in 1:20){
  arrows(x0 = i, x1 = i, y0 = samp_mn[i], y1 = samp_mn[i] - (1.96 * samp_se[i]), 
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.1);
  arrows(x0 = i, x1 = i, y0 = samp_mn[i], y1 = samp_mn[i] + (1.96 * samp_se[i]),
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.1);
}
text(x = 4, y = 7.88, labels = "Population mean = 7.2", cex = 1.75, 
     col = "blue");
```


## Confidence in a sampled mean value (99%)

```{r, echo = FALSE}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
plot(x = 1:20, y = samp_mn, ylim = c(6.3, 7.9), pch = 20, cex = 3, xaxt = "n",
     xlab = "Sample from the population (N = 40)", ylab = "Mean of the sample",
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2);
axis(side = 1, at = 1:20, labels = 1:20, cex.lab = 1.75);
abline(h = 7.2, lwd = 3, lty = "dashed", col = "blue");
for(i in 1:20){
  arrows(x0 = i, x1 = i, y0 = samp_mn[i], y1 = samp_mn[i] - (2.58 * samp_se[i]), 
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.1);
  arrows(x0 = i, x1 = i, y0 = samp_mn[i], y1 = samp_mn[i] + (2.58 * samp_se[i]),
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.1);
}
text(x = 4, y = 7.88, labels = "Population mean = 7.2", cex = 1.75, 
     col = "blue");
```

## Confidence in a sampled mean value


\Huge 

**Tools used to calculate CIs:**

- Distribution probabilities (e.g., z-scores)
- The standard error ($s/\sqrt{N}$)


## Concrete example: mean of 7 temperatures ($^{\circ} C$) {.t}

\LARGE

| $x_{1}$ | $x_{2}$ | $x_{3}$ | $x_{4}$ | $x_{5}$ | $x_{6}$ | $x_{7}$ |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| 17.1    | 15.2    |  14.9   | 12.6    | 15.2    |  10.3   | 12.7    |

Table: Seven values ($x$) of soil temperature ($^{\circ} C$) at a site

\Huge
\vspace{-4mm}

$$\bar{x} = 14$$

\vspace{2mm}

95\% Confidence in $\bar{x}$?


## Sample mean could be far off the population mean

```{r, echo = FALSE}
dat <- c(17.1, 15.2, 14.9, 12.6, 15.2, 10.3, 12.7);
mnd <- mean(dat);
```

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "An example histogram is shown with a somewhat normal shape. The mean of the distribution is indicated with an arrow.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
fig_fruits  <- read.csv(file = "data/fig_fruits.csv");
par(mar = c(6, 6, 1, 1));
hist(x = dat, xlab = "Temperature (degrees C)", 
     ylab = "Frequency", cex.lab = 2, col = "blue", main = "", cex.axis = 2,
     breaks = 10, ylim = c(0, 2.5));
mean_fig_fruit_width <- mean(dat, na.rm = TRUE);
arrows(x0 = mean_fig_fruit_width, x1 = mean_fig_fruit_width,
       y0 = 1.8, y1 = 0, lwd = 3);
mbox <- function(x0, x1, y0, y1){
    xx <- seq(from=x0, to=x1, length.out = 100);
    yy <- seq(from=y0, to=y1, length.out = 100);
    xd <- c(rep(x0, 100), xx, rep(x1,100), rev(xx));
    yd <- c(yy, rep(y1,100), rev(yy), rep(y0, 100));
    return(list(x=xd, y=yd));
}
tbox <- mbox(x0 = mean_fig_fruit_width - 0.6, 
             x1 = mean_fig_fruit_width + 0.6, y0 = 1.8, y1 = 2.2);
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="black", col="white");
text(x = mean_fig_fruit_width, y = 2, cex = 2, labels = "Mean");
```



## Concrete example: mean of 7 temperatures ($^{\circ} C$) {.t}

\LARGE

| $x_{1}$ | $x_{2}$ | $x_{3}$ | $x_{4}$ | $x_{5}$ | $x_{6}$ | $x_{7}$ |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| 17.1    | 15.2    |  14.9   | 12.6    | 15.2    |  10.3   | 12.7    |


\Huge
\vspace{-4mm}

$$\bar{x} = 14\:\pm1.6740$$

\vspace{6mm}

95\% CIs: (12.34, 15.67)



## Confidence intervals (95% CIs) around mean of 14 cm

```{r, echo = FALSE}
dat <- c(17.1, 15.2, 14.9, 12.6, 15.2, 10.3, 12.7);
mnd <- mean(dat);
```

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "An example histogram is shown with a somewhat normal shape. The mean of the distribution is indicated with an arrow.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
fig_fruits  <- read.csv(file = "data/fig_fruits.csv");
par(mar = c(6, 6, 1, 1));
hist(x = dat, xlab = "Temperature (degrees C)", 
     ylab = "Frequency", cex.lab = 2, col = "blue", main = "", cex.axis = 2,
     breaks = 10, ylim = c(0, 2.5));
mean_fig_fruit_width <- mean(dat, na.rm = TRUE);
arrows(x0 = mean_fig_fruit_width, x1 = mean_fig_fruit_width,
       y0 = 1.8, y1 = 0, lwd = 3);
mbox <- function(x0, x1, y0, y1){
    xx <- seq(from=x0, to=x1, length.out = 100);
    yy <- seq(from=y0, to=y1, length.out = 100);
    xd <- c(rep(x0, 100), xx, rep(x1,100), rev(xx));
    yd <- c(yy, rep(y1,100), rev(yy), rep(y0, 100));
    return(list(x=xd, y=yd));
}
tbox <- mbox(x0 = mean_fig_fruit_width - 0.6, 
             x1 = mean_fig_fruit_width + 0.6, y0 = 1.8, y1 = 2.2);
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="black", col="white");
text(x = mean_fig_fruit_width, y = 2, cex = 2, labels = "Mean");

arrows(x0 = 12.32592, x1 = 12.32592, y0 = 0.4, y1 = 0, lwd = 3);
tbox <- mbox(x0 = 12.32592 - 0.6, 
             x1 = 12.32592 + 0.6, y0 = 0.4, y1 = 0.8);
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="black", col="white");
text(x = 12.32592, y = 0.6, cex = 2, labels = "LCI");

arrows(x0 = 15.67408, x1 = 15.67408, y0 = 0.4, y1 = 0, lwd = 3);
tbox <- mbox(x0 = 15.67408 - 0.6, 
             x1 = 15.67408 + 0.6, y0 = 0.4, y1 = 0.8);
polygon(x=tbox$x, y=tbox$y, lwd=3, border="black", col="white");
text(x = 15.67408, y = 0.6, cex = 2, labels = "UCI");
```


## What do we actually mean by 95% confidence?

\LARGE

| $x_{1}$ | $x_{2}$ | $x_{3}$ | $x_{4}$ | $x_{5}$ | $x_{6}$ | $x_{7}$ |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| 17.1    | 15.2    |  14.9   | 12.6    | 15.2    |  10.3   | 12.7    |

- If we were to repeat our sample (N = 7) and calculate CIs over and over again, 95\% of the time our intervals would contain the population mean (which we cannot know)

- Not (quite) the same as 95\% probability that the mean is between our CIs


## But what are we actually calculating?

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A plot of a bell curve, with a range of the curve shaded in grey between negative sigma and positive sigma, symmetrical around the peak of the curve", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
xx       <- seq(from = -5, to = 5, by = 0.0001);
pr_norm  <- dnorm(x = xx, mean = 0, sd = 1);
plot(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", ylim = c(0, 0.48), xaxt = "n",
     ylab = "Probability density",  xlab = "x", lwd = 3, xlim = c(-3, 3),
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, yaxs = "i");
xx_vals <- 40001:60001;
polygon(c(xx[xx_vals], xx[60001], xx[40001]), 
        c(pr_norm[xx_vals], 0, 0), col="blue");
yy_mean1 <- seq(from = 0, to = 0.15, length = 1000);
xx_mean1 <- rep(0, times = 1000);
yy_mean2 <- seq(from = 0.25, to = max(pr_norm), length = 1000);
xx_mean2 <- rep(0, times = 1000);
points(x = xx_mean1, y = yy_mean1, type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = "dotted");
points(x = xx_mean2, y = yy_mean2, type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = "dotted");
distr_lab <- c(expression(mu-sigma), expression(mu), expression(mu+sigma));
axis(side = 1, at = c(-1, 0, 1), labels = distr_lab, cex.axis = 2);
text(x = 0,  y = 0.2, labels = "68.2%", cex = 3);
```


\footnotetext[1]{\href{https://bradduthie.github.io/stats/app/zandp/}{https://bradduthie.github.io/stats/app/zandp/}}



## But what are we actually calculating?

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A plot of a bell curve, with a range of the curve shaded in grey between negative sigma and positive sigma, symmetrical around the peak of the curve", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
xx       <- seq(from = -5, to = 5, by = 0.0001);
pr_norm  <- dnorm(x = xx, mean = 0, sd = 1);
plot(x = xx, y = pr_norm, type = "l", ylim = c(0, 0.48), xaxt = "n",
     ylab = "Probability density",  xlab = "x", lwd = 3, xlim = c(-3, 3),
     cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, yaxs = "i");
xx_vals <- 30001:70001;
polygon(c(xx[xx_vals], xx[70001], xx[30001]), 
        c(pr_norm[xx_vals], 0, 0), col="blue");
yy_mean1 <- seq(from = 0, to = 0.15, length = 1000);
xx_mean1 <- rep(0, times = 1000);
yy_mean2 <- seq(from = 0.25, to = max(pr_norm), length = 1000);
xx_mean2 <- rep(0, times = 1000);
points(x = xx_mean1, y = yy_mean1, type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = "dotted");
points(x = xx_mean2, y = yy_mean2, type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = "dotted");
distr_lab <- c(expression(mu-1.96*sigma), expression(mu), expression(mu+1.96*sigma));
axis(side = 1, at = c(-2, 0, 2), labels = distr_lab, cex.axis = 2);
text(x = 0,  y = 0.2, labels = "95%", cex = 3);
```


\footnotetext[1]{\href{https://bradduthie.github.io/stats/app/zandp/}{https://bradduthie.github.io/stats/app/zandp/}}


## How do we calculate confidence intervals?

\LARGE

**General idea: Lower (LCI) and upper (UCI) confidence interval**

\vspace{4mm}

**Verbally**

- LCI = Mean -- (z-score $\times$ standard error)
- UCI = Mean + (z-score $\times$ standard error)

\vspace{2mm}

**Mathematically**

- LCI = $\bar{x} - z \left(\frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}\right)$
- UCI = $\bar{x} + z \left(\frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}\right)$


## How do we calculate confidence intervals? {.t}

\LARGE

| $x_{1}$ | $x_{2}$ | $x_{3}$ | $x_{4}$ | $x_{5}$ | $x_{6}$ | $x_{7}$ |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| 17.1    | 15.2    |  14.9   | 12.6    | 15.2    |  10.3   | 12.7    |

\vspace{-6mm}

$N = 7$ \hspace{4mm} $\bar{x} = 14$ \hspace{4mm} $s = 2.26$ \hspace{4mm} $z = 1.96$

\vspace{2mm}
\hrule 
\vspace{2mm}
\Large
\begin{align*}
LCI &= \bar{x} - z\left(\frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}\right) &&&&&& \\ \\
    &= 14 - 1.96\left(\frac{2.26}{\sqrt{7}} \right) &&&&&& \\  
    &= 12.34 &&&&&&
\end{align*}



## How do we calculate confidence intervals? {.t}

\LARGE

| $x_{1}$ | $x_{2}$ | $x_{3}$ | $x_{4}$ | $x_{5}$ | $x_{6}$ | $x_{7}$ |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| 17.1    | 15.2    |  14.9   | 12.6    | 15.2    |  10.3   | 12.7    |

\vspace{-6mm}

$N = 7$ \hspace{4mm} $\bar{x} = 14$ \hspace{4mm} $s = 2.26$ \hspace{4mm} $z = 1.96$

\vspace{2mm}
\hrule 
\vspace{2mm}
\Large
\begin{align*}
UCI &= \bar{x} + z\left(\frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}\right) &&&&&& \\ \\
    &= 14 + 1.96\left(\frac{2.26}{\sqrt{7}} \right) &&&&&& \\  
    &= 15.67 &&&&&&
\end{align*}



## How do we calculate confidence intervals? {.t}

\LARGE

| $x_{1}$ | $x_{2}$ | $x_{3}$ | $x_{4}$ | $x_{5}$ | $x_{6}$ | $x_{7}$ |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| 17.1    | 15.2    |  14.9   | 12.6    | 15.2    |  10.3   | 12.7    |

\vspace{-6mm}

$N = 7$ \hspace{4mm} $\bar{x} = 14$ \hspace{4mm} $s = 2.26$ \hspace{4mm} $z = 1.96$

\vspace{2mm}
\hrule 
\vspace{2mm}

\Huge
\vspace{10mm}

$LCI = 12.34$ \hspace{10mm} $UCI = 15.67$



## Visualising a confidence interval

```{r, echo = FALSE}
mean_temp <- c(mean(dat), 0, 0, 0, 0, 0);
names(mean_temp) <- c("A", "", "", "", "", "");
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
barplot(height = mean_temp, ylab = "Mean soil temperature (degrees C)", 
        xlab = "Site",
        col = "blue", cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, ylim = c(0, 20),
        cex.names = 2);
arrows(x0 = 0.7, x1 = 0.7, y0 = mean_temp[1], y1 = 12.34, lwd = 4, angle = 90, 
       length = 0.3);
arrows(x0 = 0.7, x1 = 0.7, y0 = mean_temp[1], y1 = 15.67, lwd = 4, angle = 90, 
       length = 0.3);
```



## Visualising a confidence interval

```{r, echo = FALSE}
rt <- runif(n = 5, min = 10, max = 17);
ci <- runif(n = 5, min = 1, max = 2);
mean_temp <- c(mean(dat), rt);
names(mean_temp) <- c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F");
par(mar = c(6, 6, 2, 2));
barplot(height = mean_temp, ylab = "Mean soil temperature (degrees C)", 
        xlab = "Site",
        col = "blue", cex.lab = 2, cex.axis = 2, ylim = c(0, 20),
        cex.names = 2);
arrows(x0 = 0.7, x1 = 0.7, y0 = mean_temp[1], y1 = 12.34, lwd = 4, angle = 90, 
       length = 0.3);
arrows(x0 = 0.7, x1 = 0.7, y0 = mean_temp[1], y1 = 15.67, lwd = 4, angle = 90, 
       length = 0.3);
for(i in 1:5){
  pos <- 0.7 + (i*1.2);
  arrows(x0 = pos, x1 = pos, y0 = rt[i], y1 = rt[i] - ci[i], 
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.3);
  arrows(x0 = pos, x1 = pos, rt[i], y1 = rt[i] + ci[i], 
         lwd = 4, angle = 90, length = 0.3);
}
```


## Accuracy of confidence intervals

\Huge

- Assumes sample means are normally distributed around the population mean
- Applies for high sample size due to central limit theorem
- What if assumption is violated?


## Confidence intervals of a proportion

\Huge

What about for proportions?

\LARGE

| Player ID	| OS      |  Dam size	 | Biodiversity	 |
|-----------|---------|------------|---------------|
| 1	        | Android |	small	     | 0	           |
| 2         | Android	| large	     | 34            |
| 3         | iPhone	| small      | 14            |
| 4	        | Android	| large	     | 72            |
| 5         | iPhone  | medium     | 3             |


CIs around the proportion of Android users?

## Confidence intervals of a proportion

\Huge

Total players: N = 74

- 56 Android
- 18 iPhone

$$Pr(Android) = 56/74 = 0.757$$


\vspace{4mm}

CIs around this proportion?


## Confidence intervals of a proportion: Wald interval

\LARGE

**Assume a normal distribution?**

- LCI = $\bar{x} - z \left(\frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}\right)$
- UCI = $\bar{x} + z \left(\frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}\right)$

\vspace{6mm}



**Standard deviation of a binomial:**

\vspace{-3mm}

$$s = \sqrt{p\left(1 - p\right)}$$

$$p = 56/74 = 0.757$$


## Confidence intervals of a proportion: Wald interval

\LARGE

**Assume a normal distribution?**

- LCI = $p - z \times SE$
- UCI = $p + z \times SE$

\vspace{6mm}

**Standard deviation of a binomial:**

\vspace{-3mm}

$$s = \sqrt{p\left(1 - p\right)}$$

$$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{\sqrt{p\left(1 - p\right)}}{\sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{p\left(1 - p\right)}{N}}$$


## Confidence intervals of a proportion: Wald interval

\LARGE

**Assume a normal distribution?**

- LCI = $p - z \times SE$
- UCI = $p + z \times SE$

\vspace{6mm}

**Standard deviation of a binomial:**

- LCI = $p - z \times \sqrt{\frac{p\left(1 - p\right)}{N}}$ \vspace{4mm}
- UCI = $p + z \times \sqrt{\frac{p\left(1 - p\right)}{N}}$



## Confidence intervals of a proportion: Wald interval

\LARGE

**Assume a normal distribution?**

- LCI = $p - z \times SE$
- UCI = $p + z \times SE$

\vspace{6mm}

**Standard deviation of a binomial:**

- LCI = $0.757 - 1.96 \sqrt{\frac{0.757\left(1 - 0.757\right)}{74}}$ \vspace{4mm}
- UCI = $0.757 + 1.96 \sqrt{\frac{0.757\left(1 - 0.757\right)}{74}}$


## Confidence intervals of a proportion: Wald interval

\LARGE

**Assume a normal distribution?**

- LCI = $p - z \times SE$
- UCI = $p + z \times SE$

\vspace{6mm}

**Standard deviation of a binomial:**

- LCI = 0.659 \vspace{4mm}
- UCI = 0.855

## Confidence intervals of a proportion: Wald interval

\LARGE

**Problems with the Wald interval**

- Wald interval is often inaccurate
- Problematic when p near 0 or 1

\pause
\vspace{6mm}

**Other ways to calculate proportion CIs**

- Compare methods with simulated data
- Clopper-Pearson method used by jamovi
- Clopper-Pearson wider than necessary
- Based on the binomial distribution


## A bigger problem with the normal distribution and CIs

\LARGE

**Assuming we know population standard deviation ($\mathbf{\sigma}$)**

- Only have *sample* estimate, s
- Need to account for this uncertainty
- Normal distribution will be biased
- t-distribution will correct for bias
